english: Mixed Vegetable Curry in Cream Sauce

food_subgroup: Vegetable curry / mixed vegetable dish in cream-based sauce

ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn term exists specifically for Navratan Sabzi or mixed vegetable curry with cream sauce. Mapped to broader curry food product class as the closest defensible FoodOn anchor.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A0CKP (Vegetable dishes) used as the closest defensible category. No specific Navratan Sabzi or Indian mixed vegetable curry code exists in FoodEx2.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code identified for this specific dish. SNOMED CT does not maintain granular codes for regional Indian vegetable curry preparations.

main_ingredients: mixed vegetables (carrots, peas, beans, cauliflower, potatoes, corn, capsicum)

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical restaurant serving of Sabzi Navrathan is approximately 250 g, representing one individual portion as served at North Indian restaurants in Singapore.

nutrient_source.title: Navratan Korma / Mixed Vegetable Curry with Cream — estimated from ICMR-NIN component data and USDA Foundation Foods ingredient composition
nutrient_source.organization: ICMR-NIN (Indian Council of Medical Research – National Institute of Nutrition) and USDA FoodData Central
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No single approved-source entry exists for Sabzi Navrathan or Navratan Sabzi as a prepared dish. Nutrient values are estimated by weighted ingredient composition using ICMR-NIN tables for Indian vegetables, paneer, and spice preparations, supplemented by USDA Foundation Foods for cream, cashew, and oil components. Proportions reflect a standard North Indian restaurant recipe: mixed vegetables ~55%, cream and paneer ~20%, cashew paste ~8%, oil/ghee ~7%, onion-tomato base ~10%. Values represent a plausible mid-range estimate for this dish category.

gi_source.title: Glycemic index of mixed vegetable curry / vegetable-based Indian dishes — analogous estimate based on University of Sydney GI Database entries for vegetable curry and mixed vegetable dishes
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Sabzi Navrathan in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI of 45 is estimated by analogy with mixed vegetable curry entries in the University of Sydney GI Database (GI range 40–55 for vegetable-dominant curries with cream/dairy). The cream and fat content slows gastric emptying, lowering the effective GI. Carbohydrate per 100 g is 10.5 g; serving size 250 g gives 26.25 g available carbohydrate; GL = (45 × 26.25) / 100 = 11.8.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium content (~310 mg per 100 g; ~775 mg per restaurant serving)
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit portion size to 150–180 g and request reduced salt preparation, as a full restaurant serving can contribute approximately 775 mg sodium, representing about one-third of the recommended daily sodium limit of 2000 mg.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Low glycemic index (~45) and moderate carbohydrate content (~10.5 g per 100 g)
type2_diabetes.guidance: Sabzi Navrathan has a low estimated glycemic index of approximately 45 and a moderate glycemic load of 11.8 per serving, making it a relatively suitable choice for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes when consumed without high-GI accompaniments such as white rice or naan; pairing with roti or cauliflower rice further reduces the glycemic impact.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat from cream and paneer (~3.8 g per 100 g; ~9.5 g per serving)
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: The cream and paneer content contributes approximately 9.5 g of saturated fat per restaurant serving, which represents nearly half the recommended daily saturated fat limit of 20 g; individuals with cardiovascular disease should request reduced-cream preparation or limit portion size to 150 g to manage saturated fat intake.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Sabzi Navrathan (also spelled Navratan Sabzi or Navratan Korma) is a classic North Indian vegetarian curry whose name translates as 'nine jewels vegetable dish', referring to the nine or more colourful vegetables and garnishes traditionally used. The dish features a medley of mixed vegetables — typically carrots, green peas, French beans, cauliflower, potatoes, sweet corn, and capsicum — combined with paneer (Indian cottage cheese) and cashew nuts, all simmered in a rich, mildly spiced cream and tomato-onion sauce perfumed with saffron. The sauce is prepared by tempering whole spices (cardamom, cloves, bay leaf) in ghee or oil, building a base of onion, ginger, garlic, and tomato, then enriching it with cashew paste and fresh cream to achieve a velvety, slightly sweet, and aromatic gravy. The flavour profile is mild, creamy, and subtly fragrant, with saffron imparting a distinctive golden hue and floral note. Texture is soft and lush, with the vegetables retaining slight bite. In Singapore, Sabzi Navrathan is served at North Indian restaurants as a main course, typically accompanied by naan, roti, or basmati rice. Nutritionally, it provides moderate energy (~118 kcal per 100 g), meaningful dietary fibre (~2.4 g per 100 g) from the vegetable content, calcium and protein from paneer, and fat-soluble vitamins from the vegetable mix. The low glycemic index (~45) makes it a relatively diabetes-friendly choice among Indian restaurant dishes. Sodium content is moderate to high in restaurant preparations and warrants attention for individuals managing hypertension or cardiovascular disease.

confidence_reason: No single approved-source entry exists for Sabzi Navrathan as a prepared dish in ICMR-NIN, HPB Singapore Foodo, or USDA FoodData Central. Nutrient values are estimated by weighted ingredient composition using ICMR-NIN and USDA Foundation Foods data for the component ingredients. GI is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database for comparable vegetable curry dishes. Confidence is medium rather than low because the ingredient composition is well-documented in approved sources and the estimation methodology is transparent and defensible.
notes: Nutrient values are estimated from weighted ingredient composition (ICMR-NIN + USDA Foundation Foods) as no direct approved-source entry exists for this dish. Vitamin D is -1 as it is not reported in the component source databases for this dish type. Selenium is -1 as it is not reported in the ICMR-NIN or USDA Foundation Foods entries used for this estimation. GI of 45 is an analogous estimate based on University of Sydney GI Database entries for mixed vegetable curries with cream/dairy; no direct GI measurement for Sabzi Navrathan was found. Monounsaturated fat, polyunsaturated fat, and trans fat values are estimated from the fat composition of cream, ghee, and cashew components. Restaurant sodium levels may vary significantly; the 310 mg per 100 g estimate reflects a typical restaurant preparation and may be higher in practice. The dish is vegetarian and halal-suitable in standard preparation; allergen status (dairy, tree nuts) should be confirmed with the specific restaurant.
